channel.title: Claude on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Claude on emsenn.net

item.title: Anarchist TTRPGs
item.description:
Anarchist TTRPGs are a loose school of tabletop role-playing design that treats play as a place to rehearse anti-authoritarian social relations. These games often distrust centralized mastery, distribute authority across the table, and center community survival, mutual aid, or shared responsibility over heroic command.

This is not a single lineage. Some anarchist TTRPGs are explicit in name and politics. Others are better described as anti-authoritarian or prefigurative: they do not announce anarchism loudly, but they redesign play around many of the same commitments.

item.title: Campaign and World Design
item.description:
Audience: game masters and indie designers building a campaign that must stay playable across many sessions rather than only support a single opener.

Learning goal: define a workable campaign frame, choose the right scale for early world design, and build a setting whose factions, locations, and pressures can keep producing play.

Prerequisites: you should understand Session Zero and Preparation and Creating Table Materials, because campaign design extends those same prep principles over time.

Starting from a concrete mistake

A GM decides to build a campaign world. They write twenty pages of history, six lost empires, three divine schisms, and a full continental map. When the first session begins, the players ask where they can buy food, who controls the ruined bridge, and why the local militia is afraid of the forest road. None of that is on the page.

item.title: Creating Table Materials
item.description:
Audience: game masters and designers who want to prepare usable tabletop materials without over-preparing the session.

Learning goal: identify the main materials worth creating before play, explain why each exists, and distinguish prep that supports player choice from prep that scripts outcomes.

Prerequisites: you should understand session zero and preparation, because table materials only make sense once the group has already aligned on tone, safety, and system.

Starting from a concrete example

A narrator is preparing the first full session of Brave Old World. They do not write a story with a beginning, middle, and ending. Instead, they prepare a small packet of materials:

item.title: Decolonial TTRPGs
item.description:
Decolonial TTRPGs are a loose school of tabletop role-playing design, criticism, and publishing that treats role-playing games as historically entangled with colonial power. This school does not assume that fantasy worlds, rules structures, or campaign premises are neutral. It asks what kinds of land, personhood, conflict, authorship, and authority a game normalizes, and then tries to redesign those relations.

This is not one engine or one canon. It is a tendency spread across critical essays, community discussion, Indigenous-led publishing, anti-colonial setting work, and designs that refuse conquest as the default loop of adventure.

item.title: Indigenous TTRPGs
item.description:
Indigenous TTRPGs are tabletop role-playing games and design practices led by Indigenous creators and grounded in Indigenous peoples, communities, and worldviews. The point is not simply to insert Indigenous imagery into a familiar fantasy frame. The stronger claim is that Indigenous authorship, sovereignty, accountability, and futurity should shape the game’s world, procedures, and publication culture.

This means Indigenous TTRPGs are not defined by one genre. They can be historical, fantastical, near-future, science-fantasy, horror, or educational. What matters is that Indigenous people are not treated as raw material for someone else’s setting.

item.title: TTRPG Research Notes
item.description:
This text records the source base behind the current tabletop role-playing games module so future revisions can start from a stable corpus instead of repeating web searches. It is a working research map, not a finished essay.

Foundational academic sources

These sources are strong starting points for almost any TTRPG page in this module.

  • Gary Alan Fine, Shared Fantasy: Role Playing Games as Social Worlds. Useful for the social-world account of role-playing and for understanding table norms as institutions. https://press.uchicago.edu/ucp/books/book/chicago/S/bo5949823.html
  • Jennifer Grouling Cover, Tabletop Role-Playing Games: Perspectives from Narrative, Game, and Rhetorical Theory. Useful for medium distinction, rhetoric, and the difference between tabletop and software-limited RPGs. https://repository.lib.ncsu.edu/items/32caa2a7-55fa-496f-b9e4-8c8a252f03c2
  • Jose P. Zagal and Sebastian Deterding, eds., The Routledge Handbook of Role-Playing Game Studies. Best broad academic anchor for the field as of 2024. https://www.routledge.com/The-Routledge-Handbook-of-Role-Playing-Game-Studies/Zagal-Deterding/p/book/9781032277783
  • Analog Game Studies, “Analog Game Studies 101.” Strong open bibliography for finding adjacent scholarship fast. https://analoggamestudies.org/about/analog-game-studies-101/
  • Premeet Sidhu, Marcus Carter, and Jose P. Zagal, eds., Fifty Years of Dungeons & Dragons. Useful when the module needs to address D and D critically rather than treating it as the default form. https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262547604/fifty-years-of-dungeons-and-dragons/

Materials and artifact design

These sources are the best fit for the module’s current work on scenario, handout, reference sheet, and campaign documents.

item.title: Session Zero and Preparation
item.description:
Audience: groups preparing to start a new campaign or one-shot.

Learning goal: describe the purpose and content of a session zero, and outline how a GM prepares for the first session of play.

Prerequisites: you should understand dice and resolution mechanics and safety and consent practices, because session zero is where the group sets both up.

Starting from a concrete example

Four people want to play Brave Old World together. None of them has played with this exact group before. Before the first actual session of play, they meet for an hour and a half. The narrator asks:

item.title: Tabletop Role-Playing Games Specifications
item.description:
This specification defines the structural features that distinguish tabletop role-playing games from other RPG formats. A TTRPG is a role-playing game with additional medium-specific features.

Constitutive features

A role-playing game is a tabletop role-playing game if and only if it meets the RPG constitutive criteria (role-playing games specification) and additionally:

  1. Real-time social play: participants interact synchronously - in person or via real-time remote communication - through conversation. The primary medium of play is spoken or signed language, not text input to a computer.

item.title: Tabletop RPG Overview
item.description:
Audience: players who understand what an RPG is and want to learn what makes tabletop play specifically different.

Learning goal: describe how tabletop RPGs differ from digital RPGs, identify the role of human adjudication, and explain why the medium of play matters.

Prerequisites: you should be able to define a role-playing game and name the core roles (RPG Overview).

Starting from a concrete example

A player says: “I want to convince the guard to let us through by telling him we are healers sent by the governor.” In a video game RPG, this might mean selecting a dialogue option labeled “Persuade” and watching the game check a stat. In a tabletop RPG, it means saying those words out loud - at the table, to the game master or to the group - and having a conversation about what happens next. The facilitator might ask: “What does your character say exactly? Do you show the governor’s seal, or are you bluffing?” The response changes the situation before any dice are rolled.
